flashcards.back: A simple sorting algorithm that builds the final sorted array one item at a time. It iterates through an array, comparing each element with those before it and inserting it into its correct position.

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of Insertion Sort in the best case?
flashcards.back: O(n) - occurs when the array is already sorted. The inner loop exits immediately, making only one comparison per element.

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of Insertion Sort in the worst case?
flashcards.back: O(n²) - occurs when the array is sorted in reverse order. Each element must be compared and shifted past all previously sorted elements.
Yes, Insertion Sort is stable because it only shifts elements that are strictly greater than the current element, preserving the relative order of equal elements.
A sorting algorithm that divides the input into sorted and unsorted regions, repeatedly selecting the smallest (or largest) element from the unsorted region and moving it to the sorted region.

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of Selection Sort for all cases?
flashcards.back: O(n²) for all cases (best, worst, and average) because the algorithm always performs the same number of comparisons regardless of input order.
No, Selection Sort is generally unstable. When swapping elements, it may change the relative order of equal elements. The unstable version swaps with the first minimum found.

A simple sorting algorithm that repeatedly steps through the list, compares adjacent elements, and swaps them if they are in the wrong order. The pass through the list is repeated until no swaps are needed.

flashcards.front: What optimization can be applied to Bubble Sort?
flashcards.back: Add a flag to track if any swaps were made in a pass. If no swaps occur, the array is already sorted and the algorithm can terminate early, improving the best case to O(n).

flashcards.front: What is the space complexity of all three sorting algorithms (Insertion, Selection, Bubble)?
flashcards.back: O(1) - all three are in-place sorting algorithms that only require a constant amount of extra memory for temporary variables.

flashcards.front: Which sorting algorithm is best for nearly sorted arrays?
flashcards.back: Insertion Sort is best for nearly sorted arrays with O(n) time complexity. It performs minimal operations when elements are close to their final positions.

flashcards.front: Why is Selection Sort not preferred despite its simple logic?
flashcards.back: Selection Sort has O(n²) time complexity for all cases, even when the array is already sorted. It performs the same number of comparisons regardless of input order.

flashcards.front: How many passes are required in Bubble Sort to sort n elements?
flashcards.back: At most n-1 passes. After each pass, the largest unsorted element 'bubbles up' to its correct position. The algorithm can terminate early if no swaps occur.
An in-place sorting algorithm uses only a constant amount of extra memory beyond the input array. All three (Insertion, Selection, Bubble) sort are in-place algorithms.

flashcards.front: What is the average time complexity of these three sorting algorithms?
flashcards.back: All three have O(n²) average time complexity. This makes them inefficient for large datasets compared to O(n log n) algorithms like Merge Sort or Quick Sort.
